questions.question: What production requirements break the prototype in-memory event loop?
options: Unicode handling and time zones
Hours-long runs with human-input waits, worker crashes losing state, 10x peak load needing horizontal scale, and exactly-once semantics for per-run billing
Slow DNS resolution
Too many print statements
questions.explanation: The four 2026 canonical answers: workflow engines with checkpoints (Temporal, LangGraph runtime), message queue + state store, actor-model per-agent queues (MegaAgent), or hand-rolled FastAPI + Postgres.

questions.question: What three properties must hold for durable execution to work?
options: HTTPS, OAuth, rate limits
Serializable state (no live connections in closures), deterministic resume, and idempotent side effects (or deduplication keys)
Docker, Kubernetes, Helm
Logging, metrics, tracing
questions.explanation: Crash → lease timeout → another worker resumes from the last checkpoint with no duplicate side effects. LangGraph checkpoints per super-step, Temporal per activity, Restate via event-sourced journals — same pattern, three implementations.

questions.question: How does LangGraph's runtime handle an agent waiting days for human input?
options: The worker thread blocks until input arrives
interrupt() persists the state and releases the worker — when input arrives, ANY worker can resume the thread_id from its checkpoint
The run is cancelled after one hour
A dedicated VM stays allocated per waiting agent
questions.explanation: Sleeping agents cost nothing: state lives in the checkpoints table (Postgres by default), keyed by thread_id. This is the reference production design as of April 2026.

questions.question: Why does async/fiber concurrency beat thread-per-job for LLM workloads?
options: Threads cannot make HTTP requests
Threads sit idle ~99% of the time waiting for tokens to stream — fibers cooperatively yield on I/O, so one process multiplexes thousands of streaming calls
Async code is easier to write
Fibers run on the GPU
questions.explanation: LLM calls are extreme I/O-bound waits. MegaAgent's per-agent producer-consumer queues (Idle/Processing/Response states, two-layer chat coordination) scaled to thousands of concurrent agents on the same insight.

questions.question: What is Bedi's counterpoint to elaborate scaling architectures?
options: Always start with Kubernetes
FastAPI + Postgres + nothing else until load proves otherwise — simple architectures go further than expected, and premature infrastructure is its own failure mode
Never use a relational database for agents
Buy a workflow engine on day one
questions.explanation: The pragmatic "start simple" rule closes the lesson: the durable-execution patterns matter, but adopting them before load demands it trades real simplicity for speculative scale.
